coredata.dc:title: Synthesis and characterization of maltol capped silver nanoparticles and their potential application as an antimicrobial agent and colorimetric sensor for cysteine
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Naqvi, Sumra
Anwer, Humera
Ahmed, Syed Waseem
Siddiqui, Asma
Shah, Muhammad Raza
Khaliq, Saima
Ahmed, Ayaz
Ali, Syed Abid
coredata.dc:description: Maltol capped silver nanoparticles (McAgNPs) were synthesized using maltol (3-hydroxy-2-methyl-4-pyrone) as reducing and capping agent. McAgNPs were characterized by Visible and FTIR (Fourier transform infrared) spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and atomic force microscopy (AFM). Bright yellow color McAgNPs showed surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band at 436 nm, spherical shape and the average size between 35 to 50 nm. McAgNPs revealed higher stability against varying storage time, temperature, pH and salt concentrations. McAgNPs were successfully utilized for the selective and highly sensitive colorimetric detection of cysteine (Cys). Addition of Cys in a solution of McAgNPs, resulted a rapid change in color from yellow to orange because of the formation of nanoaggregates as confirmed by Visible/FTIR spectroscopy, DLS, and AFM studies. The estimated limit of detection (0.043 μM) was found to be more sensitive than previously reported other optical methods. The practical applicability of probe was also established by spiking the known concentrations of Cys in biological (blood plasma and urine) and environmental (tap and lake water) samples with significant recovery rates (92–104.6%). Despite being nontoxic to various tested cell lines, McAgNPs demonstrated potent antimicrobial, antibiofilm, and biofilm eradicating activities, thus potentially valuable in diagnostics and/or the synthesis of other nanocomposite material for broader applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Antimicrobials
Colorimetric sensor
Cysteine
Maltol
Nanoparticles

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability assessment of the synthesized McAgNPs
Antimicrobial, antibiofilm and biofilm eradicating activities
Detection of Cys and its mechanism
Preparation of biological and environmental samples
Antimicrobial, antibiofilm, and biofilm eradicating potential of McAgNPs
Effect of pH and salt on the interaction of Cys-McAgNPs complex
Interaction mechanism of McAgNPs with Cys
Size and shape analysis of the aggregates by AFM and DLS
Effect of time on the aggregation of Cys-McAgNPs
Detection of Cys in biological and environmental samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis and characterization of McAgNPs. (a) Visible spectrum and photographic image of McAgNPs solution (Insert). (b) FTIR spectra of maltol and McAgNPs.
Topographical image by AFM (a) and radius of hydration and polydispersity index was illustrated by dynamic light scattering plot (b).

(a) Visible spectra of McAgNPs collected in the presence of different amino acids and photographic image showing the colorimetric detection (Insert). (b) Visible spectra of McAgNPs in the presence of variable concentrations of Cys (0.1–200 μM). Where arrow indicates μM concentrations of Cys from a to o; a (0.1), b (0.2), c (0.3), d (0.4), e (0.5), f (0.6), g (0.7), h (0.8), i (0.9), j (1), k (40), l (80), m (120), n (160), and o (200), respectively. (Insert) Regression line showing the least senstive area of linearity (i.e. 0.1–1 μM) for the quantitative determination of Cys.
Job's plot illustrating the interaction mole fraction of Cys and McAgNPs.
The stability of Cys-McAgNPs complex. The effect of pH (a) and the different concentrations of salt i.e. NaCl (b).
Interaction mechanism of Cys with McAgNPs. (a) Comparative FTIR spectra of Cys and Cys-McAgNPs. (b) AFM topographical image of McAgNPs after interacting with Cys.

(a) The mechanism of McAgNPs interaction with Cys followed by DLS illustrating a time dependent Cys induced nanoparticles aggregation. (b) Effect of time on the Visible spectra of McAgNPs in the presence of Cys. Insert: shows the immediate color change and visible aggregates.
Schematic illustration of the strategy for the synthesis, chemosensing and probable mechanism of Cys induced aggregation of McAgNPs. Herein, the size and bond lengths are not “scaled”.
Antimicrobial, antifungal, biofilm inhibition and biofilm eradicating activities of McAgNPs.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: MBEC = Minimum biofilm eradication concentration ND = Not determine IA = Inactive.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the established McAgNPs based probe for the detection of Cys with other previously reported methods.
McAgNPs based detection and recovery studies of the Cys from biological (blood plasma and urine) and environmental (tap and lake water) samples.

head.ce:title: Synthesis and characterization of maltol capped silver nanoparticles and their potential application as an antimicrobial agent and colorimetric sensor for cysteine

International Center for Chemical and Biological Sciences (ICCBS)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Dr. Panjwani Center for Molecular Medicine and Drug Research, International Center for Chemical and Biological Sciences (ICCBS), University of Karachi, Karachi-75270, Pakistan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Maltol capped silver nanoparticles (McAgNPs) were synthesized using maltol (3-hydroxy-2-methyl-4-pyrone) as reducing and capping agent. McAgNPs were characterized by Visible and FTIR (Fourier transform infrared) spectroscopy, dynamic light scattering (DLS), and atomic force microscopy (AFM). Bright yellow color McAgNPs showed surface plasmon resonance (SPR) band at 436 nm, spherical shape and the average size between 35 to 50 nm. McAgNPs revealed higher stability against varying storage time, temperature, pH and salt concentrations. McAgNPs were successfully utilized for the selective and highly sensitive colorimetric detection of cysteine (Cys). Addition of Cys in a solution of McAgNPs, resulted a rapid change in color from yellow to orange because of the formation of nanoaggregates as confirmed by Visible/FTIR spectroscopy, DLS, and AFM studies. The estimated limit of detection (0.043 μM) was found to be more sensitive than previously reported other optical methods. The practical applicability of probe was also established by spiking the known concentrations of Cys in biological (blood plasma and urine) and environmental (tap and lake water) samples with significant recovery rates (92–104.6%). Despite being nontoxic to various tested cell lines, McAgNPs demonstrated potent antimicrobial, antibiofilm, and biofilm eradicating activities, thus potentially valuable in diagnostics and/or the synthesis of other nanocomposite material for broader applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Cysteine (Cys) is an essential sulfur containing amino acid, which performs very important role in living organisms by furnishing the cross-linking of proteins via disulfide bridges to stabilize their structure and function [1]. An elevated level of Cys has been associated with potential neurotoxicity and has already been considered as biomarker for various medical conditions and/or disorders related to metabolism [2]. Conversely, Cys deficiency is involved in various complications such as slow developmental growth, lethargy, edema, hair depigmentation, liver damage, leucocyte deficiency, hematopoiesis decline, psoriasis, skin lesions, and even neurological disorders, like Alzheimer's and Parkinson's disease [3,4]. Recently, Cys has also been investigated concerning its role in obesity, despite being extensively used in pharmaceutical products, like antibiotics and skin care preparations [5].

Generally, in human plasma, Cys is present in the range of micromolar (μM) to millimolar (mM) concentrations [6]. Because of the aforementioned importance of Cys in living system, selective and perceptive recognition towards Cys is recently a hot area of research [7]. A variety of methods such as spectroscopy, chromatography [8], capillary electrophoresis [9], immunoassay [10], electrochemical techniques [11], colorimetric and fluorescence-based techniques [12] have been established to detect Cys. Although majority of protocols mentioned can offer high sensitivity and selectivity, they are relatively complicated, expensive, and comprised of time-consuming pretreatment steps that limit their further applications. Moreover, the selective recognition of Cys over other amino acids and biomolecules (e.g. glutathione, homocysteine, proteins etc.) is an immensely important consideration. Consequently, it is essential to develop a simple, innovative, and convenient procedure for the recognition of Cys with higher sensitivity and selectivity [13,14].

The unique properties of nanomaterials (like nanoparticles) have been developed in a variety of promising applications such as renewable energy, cosmetics, medicine, biosensors, biomedical devices, and environmental remediation etc. Among them, silver nanoparticles (AgNPs) have appealed growing interest owing to their exceptional chemical, physical and biological properties compared with their macro-scale counterparts [15]. Several approaches have been used for the synthesis of AgNPs including biological/chemical reduction, microwave, radiation-assisted, solvothermal, microemulsion, ultrasound assisted, and laser ablation [16–22]. Current progress in nanotechnology, colorimetric sensing established by using metallic nanoparticles is quite an encouraging technique for biochemical investigations, because protocol of this type delivers modest information (often visible with the naked eye), as well as being sensitive and low cost [23]. Among the resources explored in the colorimetric assay, AgNPs gain significant attention because of their features of surface plasmon resonance (SPR), chemical stability, and ease of production. Additionally, the surfaces of these AgNPs can be further modified through synthesis [24]. By virtue of their exceptional visual properties i.e. their projecting color variations regulated by size reliant on SPR absorption, surface-functionalized AgNPs have been fabricated as colorimetric probes for the recognition of a wide variety of analytes, including proteins, DNA, small molecules and metal ions [25,26]. Substantial developments have also been made in the development of colorimetric detectors for the recognition of Cys [23].

In the present work, a simple method for the synthesis of AgNPs using maltol as a stabilizing and reducing agent is introduced. Maltol (3-hydroxy-2-methyl-4-pyrone) is a nontoxic (LD50 = 1410 mg/kg) chelating agent reported from wide natural resources. Maltol (C6H6O3) is an oxygen donor and bidentate ligand with only one ionizable proton (pKa = 8.36). Many researchers have taken particular interest in this ligand due to its nontoxicity and its approval to be used as a flavoring agent [27]. Maltol is also well known for its robust biological activity and forming complexes with metal ions, few of them already being verified as innovative drugs [28]. The promising ones are complexes of maltol with vanadium (insulin-mimetic properties) [29] and iron (III) ions [30]. Maltol has also been recommended as an oral mediator that can be utilized to eradicate the excess of ions in hemochromatosis [31].

Herein, we have proposed a simple, fast and cost effective colorimetric chemosensing platform for Cys detection using Maltol capped AgNPs (McAgNPs). The developed colorimetric method (Scheme 1 ) is based on the robust interaction among the Ag and SH group of Cys and thus forming AgS bond. The Cys binds on the surface of McAgNPs, which results in a high degree of aggregation as confirmed by Visible, FTIR spectroscopy, AFM and DLS analysis. The proposed method is practically applicable for the detection of Cys in biological (such as plasma, urine) and environmental (tap and lake water) samples with quite significant recovery rates of 92–104.6%.

Maltol (3-hydroxy-2-methyl-4-pyrone), silver nitrate (AgNO3) and amino acids (namely lysine HCl, glycine, arginine, valine, histidine HCl, leucine, serine, cysteine HCl, methionine, threonine, alanine, phenylalanine, isoleucine, and glutamic acid) were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich, (USA). Sodium hydroxide, sodium chloride, and all other metal salts were purchased from Merck (Germany). Deionized water (D.I water; 18.0 mΩ obtained by a Milli-Q system, Millipore, USA) was used throughout the experiment.

Absorption spectra were recorded by Visible spectrophotometer (Jenway 6310, Japan) with a resolution of 2 nm by using a quartz cell of 1 cm path length and a wavelength range of 320–820 nm. Absorption spectra of McAgNPs were recorded after dilution (if essential) in D.I water in order to set up the link between localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) signal and McAgNPs concentration.

Prestige-21 IR spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) was used for recording the FTIR spectra. FTIR spectra were recorded to identify the possible functional groups of maltol, involved in the synthesis of McAgNPs and to check the binding of Cys on the nanoparticles surface. Maltol was recorded as such, while McAgNPs and McAgNPs-Cys complex were first dried overnight in a Beschickung incubator (model 100–800; Memmert, UK) at ~40 °C for measurements.

Morphology and size of the McAgNPs was measured by an atomic force microscope (AFM model 5500 Agilent, USA) using picoview 1.122 software in tapping mode 20 μL of freshly prepared nanoparticles were placed on the triangular soft silicon nitride surface and the solution finally dehydrated in the air. The topographical images were collected instantly by tapping mode as recently described by us [32].

The hydrodynamic radius distribution of McAgNPs and their interaction with Cys were studied by Laser Spectroscatter-201 system (RiNA GmbH Berlin, Germany), furnished with He-Ne laser, 690 nm light source and an output power in the range of 10–50 mW. Investigation for autocorrelation function was performed with the program CONTIN to achieve hydrodynamic radius (RH) distributions. All experiments were performed with an autopiloted run of 50 measurements at 26 °C (20 s for single measurement) with an interval time of 1 s [16].

All the glassware was washed carefully with tap water and then rinsed D.I water, and desiccated in the oven. The AgNPs were synthesized using maltol as reducing as well as capping agent. To determine the optimum conditions, reactions with different ratios of AgNO3 to maltol were tested. Among all the ratios, AgNO3: maltol (5:1, v/v) was found to be the optimum ratio for the synthesis of McAgNPs. NaOH and HCl (0.1 M) were used to maintain the optimum pH. Similarly, reaction time was also optimized by varying the stirring time from 5 to 15 min.

For the synthesis of McAgNPs, 50 mL of 0.3 mM AgNO3 solution was boiled on the hot plate in Erlenmeyer flask covered with a watch glass, with constant stirring. Once the solution boiled, heating was stopped and 10 mL of alkaline maltol (pH of maltol solution was maintained up to 11 with NaOH solution) was added dropwise with vigorous and constant stirring (15 min) until the solution turned bright yellow indicating the formation of McAgNPs. The synthesized AgNPs were stored at room temperature (28 ± 2 °C) for further use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability assessment of the synthesized McAgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Stability of the developed McAgNPs was measured by varying different parameters such as temperatures, time, pH and salt concentrations. To check the temperature's effect on the stability, McAgNPs were exposed to heating at different temperatures, like 20 °C, room temperature (28 ± 2 °C) and 100 °C for 1 h. The shelf life of McAgNPs was monitored by storing the colloidal AgNPs solution at room temperature for several months. The effect of salt (NaCl) on McAgNPs was checked by adding various concentrations of NaCl (0.3–2000 mM) in 1:1(v/v) ratio at room temperature. Effect of pH on the McAgNPs was monitored by varying pH from 1 to 13. HCl and NaOH (0.1 M) solutions were used to maintain the pH of the nanoparticles. The Visible spectra were collected between 320 and 820 nm after treating with varying conditions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial, antibiofilm and biofilm eradicating activities
ce:section.ce:para: Antimicrobial, antibiofilm, and preformed biofilm eradicating activities of the McAgNPs were established by using standard crystal violet assay. Cellular toxicity was also established using both normal mouse fibroblast (3 t3) and malignant non-small lung carcinoma (H460) cell lines by MTT method as recently described by us [16].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys and its mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: The chemosensing capability of McAgNPs against various amino acids (including lysine, glycine, arginine, valine, histidine, leucine, serine, Cys, methionine, threonine, alanine, phenylalanine, isoleucine and glutamic acid) were analyzed. To examine the potential amino acid sensing, various amino acid solutions were taken in Eppendorf tubes in 1:1(v/v) ratio with McAgNPs. In brief, 1 mL 300 μM of different amino acids were added one by one into 1 mL of McAgNPs and incubated for 15 min at room temperature (28 ± 2 °C). Initial interactions were verified via Visible spectroscopy. The Job's plot method was performed to confirm the molar ratio of interaction between Cys and McAgNPs.

A time dependent interaction of Cys and McAgNPs was monitored by DLS, at room temperature (n = 50) as mentioned above, by mixing equal volume of McAgNPs and Cys in order to investigate the variable pattern of aggregation. Moreover, the colorimetric selectivity of McAgNPs towards Cys was further established by adding a fixed concentration of McAgNPs (1 mL) with a mixture of 200 μM of Cys (1 mL) and 200 μM of other interfering materials (i.e., amino acids and metal ions) 1 mL and observed by Visible spectroscopy. Similarly, the effect of variable pH, NaCl concentrations, and incubation time was also studied by the above mentioned method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of biological and environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para:
The urine sample was collected from Dow Diagnostic Laboratory, Karachi, Pakistan. The sample was used and analyzed immediately after the collection. The human blood plasma sample was obtained from the Center of Bioequivalence Studies and Clinical Research (CBSCR), University of Karachi, Pakistan. The biological samples were diluted 10 times separately in D.I water and 1 mL of each sample was than separately added to the variable concentration of 1 mL Cys in the presence of 1 mL McAgNPs. After 15 min of incubation at room temperature, the Visible spectra of the solutions were recorded for recovery studies. Likewise, the tap water was obtained from the research laboratory of Federal Urdu University of Arts, Science, and Technology, Karachi Pakistan. Lake water was collected from lake Kenjhar, Sindh Pakistan. Samples were used without any additional treatment. 1 mL of the lake and tap water were spiked in 1 mL of the variable concentrations of Cys in the presence of 1 mL McAgNPs, and incubated for 15 min at room temperature (28 ± 2 °C). The following formula was used for the calculation of recovery [5].
% Recovery =
found
Cys
added
Cys
× 100

All presented data were shown as the mean (±) standard deviation (SD) of the triplicate experiments. One-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was used (if required) to compare data sets, whereas p-value < 0.05 was reported as significant.

Visible spectroscopy is one of the most significant and modest techniques used to examine the optical properties of the metallic nanoparticles because absorbance is sensitive to particle shape and size [33]. In the present study, McAgNPs were synthesized by chemical reduction method using maltol as reducing as well as capping agent. After addition of alkaline maltol, the color of the reaction mixture changed from colorless to yellow, which indicated the formation of McAgNPs. The appearance of yellow color was the sign of AgNPs production, termed as surface plasmon absorbance, and arose owing to the transmission of free electrons throughout the nanomaterial surface [34]. The spectrum of the synthesized McAgNPs displayed a sharp peak at 436 nm, reflecting the uniformity of the spherical shape of the silver nanoparticles (Fig. 1a).

Infrared spectroscopy mainly FTIR allows the detection of functional groups and adsorbed molecules at the surface of nanoparticles, as well as monitoring changes of the surface chemistry. Moreover, diverse ligands attached to nanoparticles may easily be recognized according to their vibrational signatures in a quick and accurate manner [35]. Thus, McAgNPs were characterized by FTIR recorded in the region of 4000–500 cm−1. The IR spectrum of maltol alone showed a characteristic peak of OH stretching at 3249.76 cm−1, while the peak at 3061.61 cm−1 was allocated to aromatic CH vibration. The band at 1652.68 cm−1 was allocated to CO, whereas the combination peaks of the CO, CC, and CO were perceived at 1612.60, 1595.88, and 1256.4 cm−1 [27]. After the synthesis of nanoparticles, OH, CH, CO, and CO peaks completely disappeared, while reduction and broadening of the other peaks were also observed. Thus, it was suggested that these aforementioned functional groups might be involved in the synthesis and stabilization of McAgNPs (Fig. 1b).

Size and shape of the McAgNPs were assessed by using AFM and DLS techniques. AFM results showed polydispersed nature of the nanoparticles and the diameter of McAgNPs were in the range of 30–40 nm (Fig. 2a). Obtained results were also complemented by DLS analysis, which revealed the radius of hydration (RH) in the range of 35–50 nm with measured 4.8% polydispersity (Fig. 2b).

The effect of temperature, time, various NaCl concentrations and pH, on the stability of McAgNPs were studied by running a series of experiments, as demonstrated in Figs. S1 and S2. The McAgNPs were found stable at room temperature (28 ± 2 °C) for several months. When McAgNPs were exposed to heating (at 100 °C for an hour), no apparent effect on SPR and neither any precipitation was observed, which suggested that McAgNPs are quite stable. In contrast, at 20 °C, quenching in the absorption intensity was observed and thus suggested the instability of McAgNPs at a lower temperature (Fig. S1a). On the other hand, McAgNPs were also monitored at different time durations from 1 day to 1 year at room temperature (Fig. S1b). When McAgNPs were kept in an amber glass vial for a year, no apparent shift in wavelength and absorption intensity was observed. The effect of different NaCl concentrations (0.3 to 2000 mM) on the stability of McAgNPs was demonstrated in Fig. S2a. The obtained result showed that when the concentration of electrolyte increased, the absorbance of McAgNPs decreased. Moreover, turbidity in McAgNPs solution was noticed after the addition of 10 mM NaCl solution. This could be associated with strong ionic interactions between Cl− and Ag+, due to which the absorption spectrum became broader. A further increase in NaCl concentration (>10 mM) significantly affected the electronic environment of nanoparticles and thus decreased the SPR band [36]. The final pH of the synthesized McAgNPs was found to be 7.3. The effect of pH was analyzed between pH 1–13, and no considerable change in absorbance was observed in the pH range of 3 to 12, which showed higher stability of McAgNPs. In contrast, at extreme pH, higher (>12) and lower (<3) ranges, the reduction in absorbance was observed and thus suggested lower stability in these ranges (Fig. S2b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antimicrobial, antibiofilm, and biofilm eradicating potential of McAgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Silver nanomaterials (AgNPs) are quite known to possess intrinsic as well as acquired antimicrobial properties because of their small size, variety of shapes and large surface-to-volume ratios [15]. Interestingly, the McAgNPs significantly reduced >75% growth of the subjected bacterial (K. pneumoniae, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, and S. epidermidis) and fungal (C. albicans) strains in the range of 0.25–8 μg mL−1 minimum inhibitory concentrations (MICs). Antimicrobial potential of the synthesized McAgNPs was quite comparable to the standard antibiotics used in this study, i.e. gentamycin sulfate and amphotericin B (Table 1 ). Biofilm inhibitory and biofilm eradicating activities of McAgNPs were also established by crystal violet staining method. McAgNPs significantly inhibited >70% biofilm formation at MIC and 2× MIC concentrations as compared to 0.5× MIC and positive control (Table 1). In comparison to biofilm inhibition, its eradication was significantly observed at MIC and 2× MIC level (i.e. >50%) against S. epidermidis and C. albicans. However, McAgNPs failed to eradicate preformed biofilms of other microbial strains. The crystal violet data also well corresponded to microscopic data, which clearly showed the eradication potential of McAgNPs against the aforementioned selected strains (Fig. S3). Obtained results demand further investigation because in clinical setup pre-formed biofilm is of serious concern, mostly centered on the rising antimicrobial resistance that requires higher concentrations of antibiotics for treatment [37].

MTT cell viability test was also performed in order to evaluate the cellular toxicity of the synthesized McAgNPs. None of the tested concentrations of McAgNPs (i.e., 1 to 200 μg mL−1) exert any effect on normal mouse fibroblast (3 t3) and malignant non-small lung carcinoma (H460) cell lines. Although the high concentrations of McAgNPs (>200 μg mL−1) revealed <50% inhibition of malignant compared to normal cell lines, which was not surprising considering the reported diverse biological activities of maltol [30].

For the colorimetric recognition, various amino acids (including lysine, glycine, arginine, valine, histidine, leucine, serine, Cys, methionine, threonine, alanine, phenylalanine, isoleucine, and glutamic acid) were interacted with McAgNPs. Only Cys showed a significant decrease in the absorbance at 436 nm and color of the solution rapidly changed from yellow to orange (Fig. 3a), while color transformation was confirmed by the alteration of LSPR absorption [38]. In contrast to other amino acids, only Cys has a unique binding site (i.e. SH) which may interact with McAgNPs through AgS bond, followed by hydrogen bonding and electrostatic interactions. This phenomenon was possibly associated with the aggregated McAgNPs in the presence of Cys (Scheme 1). It was also well recognized that Cys binds to the surface of metallic nanoparticles and their clusters through AgS bond and this interaction was sufficient to immobilize the SH groups [39,40], thus a strong interaction between the surface of McAgNPs and the SH group of Cys might be speculated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH and salt on the interaction of Cys-McAgNPs complex
ce:section.ce:para: The analytical applications of nanoparticles are chiefly reliant on its strength. Thus, pH is a significant factor that plays an essential role in the colorimetric detection of the target [43]. The influence of pH on the McAgNPs was studied in the presence of Cys by changing the pH of the solution between pH 1–13. The McAgNPs were quite stable in the pH range of 3–12, while the preliminary pH of Cys plus McAgNPs complex (1:1 ratio) solution was measured to be 4.85. The results revealed that the degree of aggregation of Cys plus McAgNPs was maximum between pH 4 to 6 which maybe because of the isoelectric point of Cys (pI = 5.07), which facilitates the aggregation [44]. The pH 7–9 did not support the aggregation of the particles. Likewise, there was no noticeable transformation in the degree of aggregation observed at pH 1–3 and 10–13 (Fig. 5a).

The addition of salt (NaCl) increases the surface charge and decreases the electrostatic repulsion of the McAgNPs, which speed up the degree of aggregation (termed as “salting-out” effect) of the nanoparticles and permit to detect the target analyte molecule with increased sensitivity [45]. The influence of NaCl on the colorimetric detection of Cys was examined by adding variable concentrations, i.e. 0.3 to 1000 mM of NaCl in 1:1 (v/v) ratio. There was a minor change in the absorption intensity of Cys-McAgNPs complex after the addition of NaCl (0.3, 1 and 5 mM). However, as the concentration of NaCl increased, the absorption intensity decreased, and SPR band shifted towards high wavelength (i.e., red shift) which specifies the high degree of aggregation and the color of solution also changed from orange to colorless (Fig. 5b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interaction mechanism of McAgNPs with Cys

ce:section.ce:para: The chemical interaction of Cys and McAgNPs was studied by FTIR spectroscopy. The IR spectrum of Cys (alone) displayed a distinctive peak of primary amine (NH2) and carboxylate ion (COO−) because Cys as amino acid exists in the form of zwitterions [46]. The spectrum showed a characteristic band at 3366.67 cm−1 corresponding to symmetric and asymmetric stretching of the amine group. A weak band of SH at 2563.93 cm−1 confirmed the presence of thiol group, while the band at 737.65 cm−1 showed CS stretching (Fig. 6a). The peak at 1621.38 and 1219.29 cm−1 showed the symmetric and asymmetric stretching of carboxylate ion (COO−), while the intense sharp band at 1735.64 cm−1 showed the CO stretching of the carboxylic group [47]. However, the complete disappearance of characteristic bands of SH and CS (at 2563.93 cm−1 and 737.65 cm−1, respectively) were observed after the interaction of Cys with McAgNPs. This confirmed the formation of AgS bond and change the site of maltol and immobilize the SH group on the surface of nanoparticles, while the Cys also attached to maltol with hydrogen bonds [48]. The shifting and broadening of the peak of carboxyl and amino group were also observed because of the alteration in the dipole moment. The shifting, broadening and elongation of other bands of Cys also confirmed the interaction and aggregation of Cys-McAgNPs with high electron density [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Size and shape analysis of the aggregates by AFM and DLS
ce:section.ce:para: For the analysis of topographical features of nanomaterials, AFM was considered as one of the most appropriate tool [16]. Thus, in order to monitor the morphology and size variations, AFM images were recorded after the interaction of Cys with McAgNPs. Size of the McAgNPs alone was found in the range of 30–40 nm, while after the interaction with Cys, size of the complexes significantly increased suggesting the aggregation or agglomeration of the McAgNPs (Fig. 6b).

Aforementioned mechanism of McAgNPs interaction with Cys was also complemented by DLS analysis, which demonstrated a time dependent McAgNPs aggregation. Aggregation kinetics were followed from zero to 45 min after addition of Cys in McAgNPs (1:1, v/v ratio), directly into the sample cell and measured continuously for polydispersity and average hydrodynamic size. The RH of McAgNPs increased from 35 to 50 to 1118 nm within 45 min, and McAgNPs transformed from unimodal to bimodal distribution because of the development of additional higher aggregates (Fig. 7a).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of time on the aggregation of Cys-McAgNPs
ce:section.ce:para: Time is the most important factor that affects the functioning of a proposed sensor. This is why, the effect of time on the aggregation of Cys-McAgNPs was also observed by the naked eye as well as by Visible spectroscopy at various time intervals (i.e. 5, 10, 15, 30, 60 and 120 min). In addition, the color of the solution changed from yellow to orange, immediately after adding Cys into McAgNPs solution, and gradually changed from orange to colorless within 60 min with the visible formation of aggregates (Fig. 7b). On the other hand, Visible spectra recorded at different time intervals revealed that as the time of incubation increases, the absorbance of the solution decreases, while the degree of aggregation significantly increased.

Amino acids are extensively distributed in biological fluids and involved in numerous biological processes such as the synthesis of fatty acids, proteins, and ketone bodies. The abnormal quantities of each amino acids in the biological system have been closely related to numerous disorders including type-2 diabetes, liver, kidney diseases and even cancer [50]. Consequently, the selective detection of a particular amino acid concentration in biological samples in the presence of other amino acids could be beneficial in diagnosis, prediction, and understanding of the diseases. It is incredibly significant for a sensor that it can selectively detect its target analyte, show discrimination for the recognition and transmission of a particular action upon the interaction [25]. Thus, the selectivity of McAgNPs towards Cys (200 μM) was also studied by adding other amino acids (200 μM) in the reaction mixture. Obtained results revealed no interference of any other amino acids in the detection of Cys in terms of time, sensitivity and selectivity (Fig. S4a).

The selectivity of McAgNPs was also studied in the presence of different metal ions, which are well-known interfering materials. 200 μM of metals such as Fe+2, Mg+2, Zn+2, Mo+6, Ni+2, Co+2, Cd+2, Ba+2, Ce+4 Ca+2, Cr+3, Cr+6, and Fe+3 were added in the presence of 200 μM of Cys, the concentration of metal ions were kept same as Cys in reaction mixture, i.e. 1:1:1 (v/v) ratio. The obtained results show that there was no change in the absorbance intensity as well as color of the solutions, except for the Fe+2 ions which significantly decreased the absorbance, caused a change in color from orange to purple (Fig. S4b), and a change in particle size from 35 to 50 to 750 nm (Fig. S5). This suggested the formation of a cross-linking product with Cys plus McAgNPs [51]. This was in contrast to the labeled-free AgNPs based Cys probes which require metal ions such as Cr+3 [52] and Ca+2 [25] for the colorimetric detection of Cys.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Cys in biological and environmental samples
ce:section.ce:para: To prove the applicability of established colorimetric probe, we have also investigated Cys in biological (human plasma and urine) and environmental (tap and lake water) samples. Human urine and plasma samples were diluted in D.I water to decrease the matrix influence before experiments, while the water samples were used as such. When Cys (300 μM) was added to biological samples in the presence of McAgNPs, the color of the solutions immediately changed from yellow to orange and the absorbance of the solutions significantly decreased, suggesting that the biological and environmental matrix did not interfere with the sensing ability and stability of McAgNPs (Fig. S6). Recovery studies were also conducted by spiking 150 and 200 μM in biological samples, and 150, 200 and 250 μM solution of Cys in environmental samples in the presence of McAgNPs in 1:1:1 (v/v) ratio. Recoveries were in the range of 92.0 to 104.66% (Table 3 ). The good recoveries of the known quantity of Cys in biological and environmental samples also demonstrated the reliability and reproducibility of the established method.

In conclusion, a very simple and stable AgNPs was synthesized using maltol as reducing and capping agent. The synthesized McAgNPs was used as nanoprobe for the selective and sensitive detection of cysteine (Cys). The assay depends upon the rapid change in color (i.e. yellow to orange) and reduction in absorbance at 436 nm (at room temperature) over a linear range of 0.1–200 μM Cys concentrations. Moreover, McAgNPs showed excellent sensitivity towards Cys (LOD = 0.043 μM) and a novel mode of interaction (AgS bond) followed by aggregation, facilitated by extensive hydrogen bonding between free NH2 and COO– groups of the silver ligated Cys. The time dependent aggregation of nanoparticles was confirmed by DLS and visible spectroscopy, and the results show that the size of the nanoparticles increases from 35 to 50 nm to 1118 nm. The practical applicability of the probe demonstrated by spiking the known concentrations of Cys in biological and environmental samples with significant recovery rates (92–104.6%) suggests its possible role in diagnostics and design of single-step Cys purification resin and composite materials. Moreover, the nontoxic, antimicrobial, antibiofilm and biofilm eradicating properties of McAgNPs in lower micromolar concentrations against fungi, and both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria were the key features of the synthesized nanomaterial.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: Authors declared no conflict of interest. All authors contributed significantly and have read and agreed to submit the manuscript to this journal.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2019.118002.
